result:
Lately I’ve been fascinated by the way a mathematical idea can seep into the world of art and become something almost entirely emotional. Take tessellations, for example. On the surface they’re entirely logical: repetitive shapes that fill a plane without gaps. But then you look at M.C. Escher’s fantastical transformations—fish morphing into birds, angels melting into demons—and suddenly that geometrical rigor takes on a psychological edge. It’s as if the brain, hungry for patterns, takes the abstract symmetry and imbues it with narrative.

There’s a similarly poetic quality in the idea of fractals. We often hear the buzzword without stopping to think about the comfort it offers. The same tiny motif repeated endlessly at different scales—shells, river deltas, tree branches, lungs. If you look closely at a fern unfurling, you notice each leaf mirrors the entire plant, but with slight variations. That gentle self-similarity feels like a metaphor for how we grow: we’re never quite the people we were yesterday, but the shape of us still echoes through every memory, every habit. In a way, your own handwriting is a fractal, a pattern that holds no matter what word you write.

This mix of precision and poetic resonance shows up in unlikely places. I’ve been reading about quasicrystals recently—the structures that defy purely periodic repetition yet still exhibit order. Their discovery rattled crystallography in the 1980s. Imagine building a tiled floor where the pattern never exactly repeats, yet it covers the space without leaving gaps. That discovery was as much a triumph for an abstract mathematician sketching Penrose tilings as it was for a materials scientist holding a strange alloy. Somehow, an idea that seemed purely theoretical found metal under a microscope. I love how that story reminds me that curiosity, even when it looks aimless, often has unanticipated intersections with the real world.

This intertwining of logical structures and aesthetic intuition also says something about us: we’re meaning-making creatures. Mathematical beauty resonates not because we’re trained to see it, but because our senses and minds are tuned to detect pattern. Art, in turn, pushes back by taking pattern and subverting it, bending it into narrative, emotion, protest. We translate symmetry into symbolism, chaos into catharsis. Maybe that’s why music—arguably the most mathematical art—can feel like both an equation and an embrace.

I sometimes imagine a collaboration between a mathematician and a painter: instead of starting with a blank canvas, they begin with a set of axioms or a specific structure. The painter interprets a theorem as color gradients, the mathematician writes proofs inspired by brushstrokes. It sounds fanciful, but collaborations like that have actually happened. The results aren’t universal masterpieces, but they do something more interesting: they redefine the boundaries of what counts as “understanding.” They suggest that mathematics doesn’t just solve problems—it can evoke wonder. And art doesn’t just express feeling—it can sharpen logical intuition.

Maybe that’s why I’m drawn to topics where the boundary between disciplines blurs a little. It reminds me that curiosity is inherently interdisciplinary; it thrives when it has room to meander. You start by doodling a simple spiral because it’s pleasing, then end up reading about logarithmic spirals and the golden ratio in ammonite fossils. Or you sketch a simple repeating triangle and stumble into the Koch snowflake, a finite perimeter enclosing infinite complexity. In those moments, math stops feeling like a set of rules and becomes a landscape, full of trails to wander.

So if I had to pick something hopeful to hold onto—and maybe this is the point of writing about whatever you want—it’s the knowledge that beauty and logic aren’t separate domains. They’re two languages describing the same human drive to make sense of the world. Whether you’re tracing the symmetry of a cathedral window or the algorithm behind a piece of generative art, you’re participating in that old, delightful dialogue between structure and surprise.
